draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond to sunrise/sunset aesthetic — energizing but comforting, not clinical
facade.label: Warm orange glow
facade.acceptImplies: Design should use warm colors (amber, coral, rust) to create motivational but approachable feeling
facade.rejectImplies: Design should skew cooler, crisper, more technical — blues and grays for clarity and modernity

evidence.hypothesis: Runners respond to sunrise/sunset aesthetic — energizing but comforting, not clinical
evidence.implication: Design should use warm colors (amber, coral, rust) to create motivational but approachable feeling

facade.hypothesis: Runners need high contrast and modern clarity for quick glance readability during movement
facade.label: Bright sans-serif, dark sky
facade.acceptImplies: User values legibility and contemporary minimalism over warmth or nostalgia
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers softer contrast, serif fonts, or a more organic/textured feel

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer a conversational, upbeat personality over corporate formality in their fitness app experience.
facade.label: Cheery voice, casual tone
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use exclamation points, encouraging language, occasional humor, and speak like a running buddy—not a weather service.
facade.rejectImplies: The app should use neutral, factual, confident tone—like a trusted authority. No emojis or casual asides. Professional and straightforward.

facade.hypothesis: Runners need persistent access to current conditions and performance metrics without leaving their route view
facade.axisTargeted: layout organization — persistent sidebar vs full-screen immersion
facade.label: Sidebar with live stats
facade.acceptImplies: users value quick-glance data access; design should include always-visible weather + pace/distance panel
facade.rejectImplies: users want minimal distraction while running; full-screen map-first layout with metrics on-demand makes sense

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer gentle, reassuring motion that signals the app is alive without feeling urgent or aggressive
facade.axisTargeted: Transition intensity — from jarring/instant to soothing/animated
facade.label: Soft pulse on start
facade.acceptImplies: Design embraces fluid micro-interactions: subtle breathing effects, ease-out animations, delayed reveals. Motion as comfort, not command.
facade.rejectImplies: Motion should be crisp and direct — runners want speed and clarity, not handholding. Snappy transitions and instant state changes feel more athletic and efficient.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV)
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive menu navigation for weather info during runs
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative atmosphere
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners care most about real-time effort level, so numeric HR data should be visually dominant and color-coded by exertion zone
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (HR metrics, zones, thresholds) vs. Key metrics (single summary number or simple visual indicator)
facade.label: Heart rate zones highlighted
facade.acceptImplies: Build the app around detailed physiological feedback—runners want to see live HR zones, threshold alerts, and zone breakdowns. Design for data density and precision.
facade.rejectImplies: Simplify to one or two key signals (current effort, go/stop recommendation). Runners don't need live HR zone granularity—they need quick guidance while moving.

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer clean, distraction-free displays during activity — information hierarchy matters more than warmth
facade.label: Crisp, minimalist interface
facade.acceptImplies: Design should strip away decorative elements, use high contrast, prioritize readability at a glance during motion
facade.rejectImplies: The warm, approachable aesthetic from the first probe remains primary — embrace texture, color, and personality over clinical efficiency

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
draft.html:

6 PM
58°
